feedback.text: Dear Commission Members, as a concerned European citizen, I respectfully urge that the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act includes robust protections for digital ownership and individual freedoms. In recent years, i have noticed an alarming trend: the increasing delegation of regulatory power to private corporations, particularly in matters of digital access and expression. This shift is occurring largely without public oversight or legal accountability. One concerning example is the growing role of payment service providers such as Visa and Mastercard, which have been restricting transactions for entirely legal digital content and services. These decisions are often based on vague internal policies or ideological criteria, rather than clear legal standards. We cannot allow private networks to determine which lawful goods and services citizens may access, without due process or democratic input. Equally worrisome are developments in the United Kingdom Online Safety Act. Its ambiguous language with its expansive enforcements have encouraged platforms to preemptively censor or restrict access to content. There is legitimate concern that similar models could be adopted within the European Union under the pretext of safety or harm reduction, that may result in a digital environment that undermines fundamental rights. Digital consumer rights need to evolve, citizens must retain the right to access the legal digital content they have purchased, the right to self-host and preserve that content, and the right to engage in lawful digital transactions free from ideological interference, I ask the Commission to do what they can to not allow private companies, guided by their inside made policies, to gain unchecked influence over what Europeans are permitted to access, purchase, or express online. A prominent example of public concern is the growing backlash against the removal of video games from digital libraries, including singleplayer or offline titles, simply because publishers have chosen to discontinue support. The "Stop Killing Games" petition, which has garnered over 1.5 million signatures, shows the publics strong desire for digital permanence and autonomy. I'm especially interested with this issue, and I want this to be the main point of this feedback. If a product is being advertised as such, when bought, it should be owned, not rented for a temporary period time, videogames publishers don't have any obligations to state to their consumers if or when their "product" is going to end, and that is a violation of basic consumers righst, but it goes beyond that. Publisher should be held accountable and make sure their sold products work even when they don't want to support them anymore. Could be done by giving the game to its community, which are also its customers, as a form of self hosting, to take care of it themselves. Could be done by giving the game to another corporate entity to host the game, and perhaps even selling it. Or something in betweens. The point is, customers should own a copy of the game and the game files once they bought it, not a license that can be revoked at any time, publishing companies should be required to prepare some kind of end-of-life plan for the games they sold, both for multiplier and singleplayer experiences. Online services such as these still need to catch up with consumers rights laws so please, take a look into this matter. -Sincerely, a concerned European citizen.
